scenario: A health insurer runs Camunda 8.8 with 200 workers spread across 8 microservices. The team annotates one handler to fetch three variable names, then configures camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>.fetch-variables with a different list of two names to scope what the broker activates for that specific worker.
question: How does fetch-variables behave when set under camunda.client.worker.override.<job-type>?

options.text: Override fetch-variables extends the annotation list, activating the union of annotation values and configured names on each job
Override fetch-variables is ignored when an annotation is present, activating only the variables named in the handler annotation
Override fetch-variables forces every process variable, activating every variable regardless of annotation or configured list
Override fetch-variables replaces the annotation list, activating only the names configured under that per-job-type override

a.text: Incorrect. Extending the annotation list is the documented defaults behaviour; under an override entry fetch-variables replaces the list, it does not extend it.

b.text: Incorrect. The override is not ignored when an annotation exists; when configured, the override entry replaces the annotation list for that job type.

c.text: Incorrect. Fetching every process variable is governed by force-fetch-all-variables, which overrides fetch-variables; the override of fetch-variables itself replaces the list.

d.text: Correct. The reference page states that when fetch-variables is set in an override, it replaces the list of variables to fetch; only the names configured under that per-job-type entry are activated for that worker.

explanation: The reference distinguishes fetch-variables semantics: under defaults the list extends what the annotation declares, but under an override entry it replaces the annotation list. The separate force-fetch-all-variables flag overrides fetch-variables when true.
